## liboqs

### PR [#2449](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2449) — Constant-time analysis framework (LFX mentorship output)

Pablo Gutiérrez presented the work carried out with Basil Hess during the LFX mentorship. The PR introduces a constant-time analysis framework for liboqs combining two dynamic testing tools:

- **Valgrind** with the Kyber/KyberSlash patch from Dan Bernstein's group, which extends Valgrind to detect variable-latency issues of the kind exposed by the KyberSlash attack.
- **Clang MemorySanitizer (MemSan)**, used to detect additional sources of secret-dependent behaviour.

The framework runs both tools across multiple compiler/compiler-version combinations, generic vs. optimised liboqs builds, and different compiler optimisation flags, in order to surface constant-time issues under any of these configurations. A new compilation macro (`OQS_ENABLE_TEST_CONSTANT_TIME_MEMSAN`) mirrors the existing Valgrind macro. Tests involving SLH-DSA / SPHINCS+ are excluded for now due to prohibitive runtime. False positives can be suppressed via dedicated suppression files; ML-KEM suppression files are included in the PR and no constant-time issues were found for ML-KEM under any configuration. A Python script is also included to parse and visualise the log output.

The CI workflow (`ct-tooling`) runs weekly and currently covers only ML-KEM variants (under one hour total). It can also be triggered manually to test other algorithms. Coverage of further algorithms will be handled in follow-on PRs, and was identified as a strong candidate for a future LFX mentorship.

Key discussion points:

- The Valgrind patch is applied at CI time by building Valgrind from source with the patch applied. Basil suggested and the group agreed that a better long-term solution is a pre-built CI container image containing the patched Valgrind, avoiding a full rebuild on every run. This should go in a separate PR against `ci-containers`. Michael noted that upstream efforts to incorporate the patch into Valgrind itself may be in progress and should be tracked.
- CI failures visible in the PR are unrelated to the constant-time work: Bruce identified the root cause as Intel's download server blocking automated access, which affects the AVX-512 emulation step used in several CI jobs. This is a known cross-cutting issue ([issue #2436](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2436)) affecting multiple open PRs.
- Michael requested that the PR include a how-to guide explaining how to create and update suppression files, and suggested merging the new framework's documentation into the existing constant-time testing directory to avoid duplication.
- The group agreed the PR's scope should be considered complete once ML-KEM suppression files and the how-to documentation are in place; further algorithm coverage proceeds via separate PRs.
- The PR is not targeted at 0.16 and is considered a candidate for 0.17.

Pablo described his experience as a mentee positively: the mentorship introduced him to the topic of constant-time analysis from scratch, combined literature review with practical testing, and was well supported by the Linux Foundation. Rodrigo highlighted this as a successful mentorship outcome and encouraged others to consider becoming mentors for future cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Pablo):** Add a how-to guide for suppression file creation and merge documentation into a single constant-time directory; this is the agreed completion criterion for [PR #2449](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2449).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce / Basil):** Open a PR against `ci-containers` to add a pre-built container image containing the patched Valgrind, avoiding repeated source builds in CI.

### PR [#2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407) — HQC update (0.16 blocker)

Bruce confirmed the HQC PR is ready for review and is waiting on reviewers. Rodrigo and Douglas have previously approved an earlier version; a minor subsequent change (a small MLDSA-related update) means those approvals need to be refreshed. Rodrigo confirmed he will re-review this week. All team members with relevant expertise are asked to review, as this remains the main blocker for the 0.16 release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo, all):** Review and (re-)approve [PR #2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407) to unblock the 0.16 release.

### PRs [#2431](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2431) and [#2460](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2460) — Security fixes for 0.16

Bruce flagged two security-related PRs targeted at 0.16:

- [PR #2431](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2431): Implement value barrier and apply to FrodoKEM.
- [PR #2460](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2460): Fix uninitialized `encaps_derand` pointer dereference for NTRU KEMs (moved out of draft during the meeting).

Both PRs have passed all KATs. Bruce and Douglas intend to merge them once CI is green; the Intel download site issue is currently blocking CI across multiple PRs. Reviews are welcome.

### PR [#2441](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2441) — Algorithm support table

Rodrigo reported that the CI issues blocking this PR were resolved during the morning of the meeting. Outstanding work includes following up with algorithm teams that have not yet responded (noting that some outreach emails may have been caught in spam filters), and resolving the categorisation of legacy entries such as Kyber and algorithms originating from the PQClean repository, which do not have a clear upstream point of contact. Rodrigo will coordinate with Douglas on the FrodoKEM entry.

### 0.16 release scope freeze

Bruce confirmed that the 0.16 milestone scope is now frozen: no new items will be added before the first release candidate. High-severity CVEs would be handled via an RC process if needed. The CI pipeline failures caused by the Intel download site are the top priority to resolve before RC1 can proceed.

### NIST on-ramp round 3 tracking issues

Rodrigo confirmed that tracking issues have been opened in liboqs for each of the five remaining NIST signature on-ramp algorithms not yet included in liboqs ([issues #2452](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2452), [#2453](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2453), [#2454](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2454), as well as the pre-existing [#2101](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2101) and [#1946](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/1946)), following the decision at the June 2 TSC meeting to target all remaining on-ramp algorithms for inclusion by 0.17.

## OQS-OpenSSL 3 provider

Rodrigo noted that [PR #778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778) (avoid encoder hotpath in P-curve hybrid KEM keygen) has been awaiting his review for several weeks. He confirmed he will review and approve this week. The approach taken in that PR — developing a standalone benchmark script to verify the performance improvement — is considered correct; integrating the script into CI is left for a follow-on PR. The long-standing [PR #641](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/641) (generate.sh cleanup) also remains open; Rodrigo noted it may contain useful work relevant to the generate script improvements discussed previously and will look at it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Review and approve [PR #778](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/778) this week.
